title: The Queen Bee
summary: Two kings' sons once set out on a grand adventure, but they fell into wild and reckless ways and never returned home.
full_text:
Two kings' sons once set out on a grand adventure, but they fell into wild and reckless ways and never returned home. Their younger brother, whom everyone called Simpleton, went to seek them. When he found them at last, they laughed at him, saying he was too simple to make his way in the world. But they continued on together, traveling through strange lands.

They came upon an anthill, and the elder brothers wanted to stir it up for sport, to watch the ants scatter in panic. But Simpleton said, "Leave the little creatures in peace. I will not allow them to be disturbed." The brothers sneered but moved on.

They reached a lake where a flock of ducks swam peacefully. The elder brothers wanted to catch and roast them, but Simpleton said, "Leave the creatures alone. I will not allow you to kill them."

Next, they found a beehive overflowing with honey. The elder brothers wanted to build a fire beneath the tree to suffocate the bees and take the honey. But Simpleton said, "Leave the creatures alone. I will not allow them to be burned."

At last, they came to a great castle, where stone horses stood in the stables and no living soul was to be seen. They explored every hall until they reached a door secured with three heavy locks, and through a small opening, they saw a tiny gray-haired man sitting at a table. They called to him three times before he rose, unlocked the door, and silently led them to a table laden with food and wine. After they ate, he showed them to their chambers.

In the morning, the gray man took the eldest brother to a stone table where three tasks were inscribed. The first task was to gather a thousand pearls hidden in the moss of the forest. If a single pearl was missing by sunset, the seeker would turn to stone. The eldest brother searched all day but found only a hundred. As the sun set, he was turned to stone.

The second brother tried the next day but found only two hundred pearls. He too became stone.

Then Simpleton took his turn. He searched through the moss slowly, finding a pearl here and another there. He grew discouraged, for the task seemed impossible. But the ants he had protected came to his aid. Hundreds of ants scuttled through the moss, finding every pearl and carrying them to a single pile. By sunset, all one thousand pearls were gathered.

The second task was to retrieve the key to the princess's chamber from the bottom of a lake. Simpleton stood at the shore, helpless. The ducks he had saved swam to him, dove into the water, and brought up the key in their beaks.

The third task was to identify the youngest and most beautiful of three sleeping princesses, who looked exactly alike. Simpleton did not know how to choose. But the queen bee he had protected flew to his side and landed on the lips of the youngest princess, for she was the sweetest of all.

Thus, Simpleton broke the castle's enchantment and freed his brothers from their stone prisons. He married the youngest princess and became king, ruling wisely and kindly, and the animals he had befriended always remembered his kindness.

title: Quetzalcoatl
summary: Quetzalcoatl, whose name means Feathered Serpent, was one of the most important deities in the Mesoamerican pantheon.
full_text:
Quetzalcoatl, whose name means Feathered Serpent, was one of the most important deities in the Mesoamerican pantheon. He was the god of wind, air, learning, and civilization. To the Aztecs and the earlier Toltecs, he was the bringer of knowledge, the inventor of the calendar, and the giver of maize to humanity.

According to Aztec mythology, Quetzalcoatl was the son of the virgin goddess Chimalman and the creator god Ometeotl. He was born as a twin, along with his brother Xolotl, the dog-headed god of lightning and death. From birth, Quetzalcoatl was destined for greatness.

He created the world not once but five times — the five suns of Aztec cosmology. Each sun represented an age of creation and destruction. Quetzalcoatl presided over the second sun, the age of wind, which was destroyed by hurricanes. In the current age, the fifth sun, he descended to Mictlan, the underworld, to gather the bones of the dead and fashion them into the first humans of this era. He mixed the bones with his own blood to give life to humanity, making himself the father of all people.

As the god of wind, Quetzalcoatl was also the patron of priests and learning. He taught humanity the arts of writing, metalworking, and astronomy. He gave them the calendar that measured time with astonishing precision and the rituals that kept the cosmos in balance. Under his guidance, the Toltec civilization flourished in the city of Tula.

But Quetzalcoatl was not without his enemies. Tezcatlipoca, the smoking mirror god of night and sorcery, was his eternal rival. Tezcatlipoca tricked Quetzalcoatl into drinking pulque, a fermented agave drink, and committing shameful acts with his sister. In disgrace, Quetzalcoatl left Tula, sailing east on a raft of serpents, promising to return.

When Hernán Cortés arrived on the shores of Mexico in 1519, the Aztec emperor Moctezuma II believed he was Quetzalcoatl returning to reclaim his kingdom. This belief contributed to the fall of the Aztec Empire. But to this day, Quetzalcoatl remains the most recognizable figure of ancient Mexico — the feathered serpent who brought knowledge, who created humanity, and who sails forever toward the sunrise.

title: Queen Abla Pokou: The Mother of the Baoule People
summary: In the 18th century, a great crisis divided the Ashanti Kingdom in what is now Ghana.
full_text:
In the 18th century, a great crisis divided the Ashanti Kingdom in what is now Ghana. A civil war forced a group of Ashanti to flee their homeland. They were led by a woman of extraordinary courage: Princess Abla Pokou, niece of the Ashanti king.

Abla Pokou led her people westward, through the dense forests of West Africa, searching for a new homeland. They traveled for months, pursued by enemies, surviving on roots and wild game. Hundreds died along the way. But Abla Pokou never wavered.

They reached the Komoe River. It was the rainy season, and the river was swollen to a raging torrent a mile wide. There were no boats, no bridges. The pursuers were closing in. The people despaired.

Abla Pokou consulted the priest. The priest communed with the spirits and delivered a terrible message: "The river will not let you pass unless you sacrifice the most precious thing you possess. You must throw your most valuable child into the water."

The people looked at each other. Which child would be chosen? Who would volunteer their son or daughter?

Abla Pokou stepped forward. She held her infant son in her arms, her only child. She looked at the raging river, then at her people. Without a word, she threw her son into the water.

The river parted. The people crossed on dry ground. On the other side, Abla Pokou turned to look back at the river where her son had drowned. She cried out: "Ba-ou-li!" — "the child is dead!"

From that cry came the name of a new people: the Baoule. Abla Pokou became the first queen of the Baoule nation. Her sacrifice became the founding story of a people.

Today, Abla Pokou is remembered as the mother of the Baoule, one of the largest ethnic groups in Ivory Coast. Her statue stands in the capital Yamoussoukro. Her story is taught in every Ivorian school. The sacrifice of her son is not seen as a tragedy but as the ultimate act of leadership — the willingness to give everything for the survival of the people.

The story of Abla Pokou raises questions that every leader must face: what are you willing to sacrifice? How much do you love your people? The answer, for Abla Pokou, was everything.
moral: True leadership requires the willingness to sacrifice everything for the people.

source_references.title: L'Islam et les Noirs de l'Afrique Occidentale: Études d'histoire et de sociologie musulmanes
source_references.author: M. Delafosse

title: Queen Crane
summary: A poor shepherd boy named Proud is befriended by a crane who is actually an enchanted queen, and through a series of magical transformations, he wins her hand and a kingdom. This very popular Northern tale features a seemingly witless hero with an unusual transformation motive.
moral: Loyalty and a pure heart see through enchanted disguises to find true royalty.
source: The Swedish Fairy Book, ed. Clara Stroebe (1921), Ch.XVII: Queen Crane. From the collection of Hyltén-Cavallius and Stephens, communicated by Dr. v. Sydow-Lund. A very popular Northern fairy-tale with a witless hero and an unusual transformation motive.

title: The Queen
summary: Queen Adelgunda possesses a remarkable gift: she can see into the hearts of men. This power makes her a wise and just ruler, but it also creates complications. When the noble Sigmund — whom the queen secretly loves — is accused of dishonorable intentions, the queen's gift suggests his guilt. To save Sigmund from the dishonor that her veiled judgment implies, Adelgunda must make a painful choice: reveal her own love for him, risking her reputation and position, or allow an innocent man to be condemned. The story follows the queen's internal struggle as she balances her public duty with her private heart. In a climactic moment, she chooses love and truth over safety and appearances, baring her soul to save the man she loves. The story is notable for its psychological depth and focus on a female protagonist who holds real power and must navigate complex emotional and political terrain. Anna Wahlenberg, who wrote over 200 fairy tales, was known for embedding social commentary and feminist themes in her stories. 'The Queen' exemplifies her ability to combine traditional fairy tale enchantment with nuanced human emotion, portraying a ruler whose supernatural gift becomes a burden that only love can truly overcome.
moral: True love requires the courage to be vulnerable and the willingness to sacrifice for another.
source: Great Swedish Fairy Tales, 1973. Originally published as 'Drottningen' (1903) by Anna Wahlenberg. Also appears in 'The Diamond Bird and Other Tales' (1969, Doubleday).

title: The Queen's Necklace
summary: The cruel King Hendrik possesses a necklace of one hundred perfect pearls. He forces his nephew to find him a wife, and the kind Blanzeflor is chosen. On their wedding day, the king fastens the pearls around her neck with a threat: the day she is found without them will be her last. Blanzeflor, trapped in a cold marriage, gives away the pearls one by one to help the needy. When the king discovers the necklace is gone, he sentences her to death, but the birds she befriended rescue her and tear the wicked king to pieces.
full_text: A cruel and greedy king named Hendrik possesses a necklace of one hundred perfect pearls, which he prizes above all his treasures. He sends his nephew, Nils, to find him a wife. Nils returns with the kind and beautiful Blanzeflor. On their wedding day, the king fastens the pearls around her neck with a terrible warning: 'The day I find you without them will be your last.' Blanzeflor, trapped in a cold marriage, finds her only happiness in feeding the birds. She begins giving away the pearls one by one to help the needy villagers — the poor, the sick, the hungry — each pearl buying food or warmth for someone desperate. When the king discovers the necklace is gone, he sentences her to death. On the morning of her execution, however, the birds she befriended come to her cell, rescue her, and tear the wicked king to pieces, freeing the villagers from his tyranny. Blanzeflor then marries her true love, Nils, and they live in peace.
moral: Kindness to the smallest creatures can bring the greatest deliverance.
source: Bland tomtar och troll, 1914 (Anna Wahlenberg). English translation in An Illustrated Treasury of Swedish Folk and Fairy Tales (Floris Books, 2019).

title: Qals (Mink) and the Transformations (Coast Salish)
summary: Qals (Mink) is a major trickster figure in Coast Salish mythology, particularly in the Fraser River region. In the longest and most important story told by George StsE'lis to Franz Boas, Qals shapes the world through his encounters with various flawed ancestors. Mink tricked a chief's daughter into sleeping with him, producing a human boy named T'qulä'tca, who became the ancestor of the Ts'uwä'l people. Qals encountered numerous beings who needed correction: he transformed a toothed-vagina woman named LEqyiles who bit off Mink's hand into stone on the Harrison River; he changed a one-legged man into stone at StsE'lis; and he transformed whole villages of people who opposed him into animals, rocks, and landmarks. The Qals cycle demonstrates the Coast Salish concept of transformation as a world-ordering force. Everything in the landscape has a story behind it — every rock, every mountain, every bend in the river — recording where Qals walked and changed the world.
source: Franz Boas texts from StsE'lis; Brian Thom, Transformer Stories

title: The Quilting Party Ghost
summary: A classic Gullah ghost story set in the Sea Islands of Georgia. A group of women gathered for a quilting party at an old cabin on St. Helena Island. As they worked on the quilt by lantern light, they told stories to pass the night. An old woman named Sarah, whose grandmother had been enslaved on the plantation before the Civil War, told them about the cabin's history. It was said that a young enslaved woman named Liza had died there, waiting for her husband who had been sold away. As Sarah finished the story, the women heard footsteps on the porch. The door creaked open, and a young woman in an old-fashioned dress stood there, looking at the quilting frame. The women froze, recognizing the description Sarah had just given. The ghost smiled sadly, touched the quilt, and vanished. After that night, the women always left a piece of blue fabric—haint blue—in the quilt pattern to protect the cabin. The story reflects the Gullah belief that ghosts are often not malevolent but are ancestral presences who remain connected to the places and activities they loved in life.
source: Gullah Geechee oral tradition; snip Sea Islands folklore collections

title: Quetzalcóatl — The Feathered Serpent
summary: Quetzalcóatl, the Feathered Serpent, is the most important syncretic deity in Mexican and Mexican-American spiritual folklore. In Aztec tradition, he was the god of wind, knowledge, learning, and the priesthood — credited with creating humanity by descending to Mictlan (the underworld) and mixing his blood with the bones of previous generations. He is also associated with the morning star (Venus) and is the inventor of books and the calendar. The feathered serpent iconography appears throughout Mesoamerica from Teotihuacan (200 BCE) to the Aztec Empire, and his worship was centered at Cholula's great pyramid. In Chicano spirituality, Quetzalcóatl has been reclaimed as a symbol of indigenous identity, cultural resistance, and the profound wisdom of pre-Columbian civilizations. The legend of Quetzalcóatl's prophesied return was later conflated with Hernán Cortés's arrival, a complex historical narrative that continues to shape Mexican-American understanding of conquest and cultural survival.

title: Queen Makeda and King Solomon
summary: In the 10th century BCE, Queen Makeda of Ethiopia (known as the Queen of Sheba) heard of King Solomon's unparalleled wisdom and journeyed to Jerusalem with a magnificent caravan laden with gold, spices, and precious stones. Solomon was captivated by her beauty and intelligence. Through clever trickery, he persuaded her to stay, and she conceived his child. Before returning to Ethiopia, Solomon gave her a ring as a sign of their covenant. Makeda gave birth to a son, Menelik I (Bayna-Lehkem), who would later travel to Jerusalem as a young man. Solomon recognized his son and anointed him king of Ethiopia. According to the Kebra Nagast, Menelik then brought the Ark of the Covenant to Ethiopia, where it remains to this day in Axum. This legendary lineage established the Solomonic dynasty that ruled Ethiopia until 1974.
moral: Wisdom and diplomacy forge alliances that echo through millennia.

title: Queen Arawelo: The Warrior Queen of Somalia
summary: Queen Arawelo (Carraweelo) was a legendary proto-Somali queen who established a matriarchal society in the Sanaag region around the 1st century CE. According to tradition, Arawelo was born to Haramaanyo and raised during a time of brutal clan warfare and devastating drought. After her husband and children died in the violence, she was cast out as a widow. In the myrrh forests of the northern mountains, she gathered other outcast women and formed a warrior sisterhood. Arawelo trained her army to restore peace, believing that women must take power to end the endless cycle of male violence. She instituted a system where women held political and economic control, and men were judged by their contributions to the community. Her reign was both feared and revered — some accounts describe her punitive justice against violent men, while others celebrate her as a wise ruler who brought peace to the land. Her story is told across the Horn of Africa as a symbol of female empowerment.
source: Somali oral tradition, Feminism and Religion journal
moral: When society fails its most vulnerable, new forms of leadership must emerge.

title: Qamata and the Creation of the Xhosa World
summary: Qamata (also known as uThixo) is the supreme creator god of the Xhosa people who existed before all things. From a primordial void of darkness and silence, Qamata created the world using Uhlanga, a bed of sacred reeds that became the material for all life. He divided existence into three realms: the sky for himself, the earth for humans, and the underworld for ancestors. From the reeds he made the first humans and gave them life, then created cattle as sacred beings representing prosperity. Qamata later fought and defeated the chaos giant Iqungqungqu, bringing order to the cosmos. He stays distant but works through ancestors and natural signs, with thunder and the sun representing his power.

source_references.title: Divination by Lot Among the Xhosa
source_references.author: W.D. Hammond-Tooke

title: Qat the Creator and the Birth of Night
summary: In the Banks Islands of Vanuatu, the principal god Qat emerged from a stone that exploded as his mother. He had eleven brothers called Tagaro and a companion named Marawa the spider. Qat carved the first three pairs of humans from dracaena wood and brought them to life by playing drums, making them dance. When his brothers tired of constant daylight, Qat traveled to the Torres Islands to purchase Night from I Qong. He brought back Night, a rooster, and an obsidian stone. His brothers lay down and slept while the sky darkened. After a while Qat had the rooster crow to wake them and sliced the dark sky apart with the obsidian, creating the first Dawn. Marawa, envious of Qat's creations, made his own figures and buried them, thus introducing death.

title: Qat and the Sky Maidens
summary: One day Qat discovered a group of sky maidens who had removed their wings to bathe. He quickly buried one pair of wings so that one maiden, Vinmara, had to remain behind. She became his wife and was called Ro-Lei. They lived happily until Qat's mother reproached her daughter-in-law and made her cry. Ro-Lei's tears washed away the earth covering her wings; snip she put them on and flew back to the sky. Qat shot an arrow with a rope tied into the roots of a great banyan tree in the sky and climbed after her. As he climbed back down with Ro-Lei, the root snapped and he plunged to his death while she flew safely away. In other versions, Qat does not die but sails away in a canoe, promising to return one day.

title: Qozy Körpesh and Bayan Sulu
summary: The oldest Kazakh love dastan, dating back over 1,500 years to the era of the Türgesh Khaganate. Qozy and Bayan are promised to each other from birth, but Qozy's father dies before the marriage can take place. Bayan's ambitious father, Karabai, breaks the promise and betroths her to a wealthy rival. Qozy searches for his beloved across the vast steppe, overcoming obstacles imposed by Bayan's family. The lovers are reunited, but a tragic fate awaits them — Qozy is murdered by Kokman, a servant of Bayan's unwanted suitor. Bayan, in her grief, avenges Qozy's death and then takes her own life to join her beloved in death. Their shared grave is marked by a twelve-meter stone mausoleum standing in the Ayagoz district of Kazakhstan.

title: Qamar al-Zaman and the Princess of the Jinn
summary: A tale from the Arabian Nights about Prince Qamar al-Zaman (Moon of the Age), son of King Shahriman, who refuses marriage and is imprisoned. The princess of the jinn, in a parallel story, also refuses marriage and is imprisoned by her father the jinn king. Through the machinations of a devious jinn and a fairy, they are magically brought together in sleep, each seeing the other in dreams. They fall in love sight unseen, leading to a quest across the realms of men and jinn to unite. The story exemplifies the supernatural romance genre of medieval Arabic literature.
